coredata.dc:title: Deep eutectic solvent-assisted preparation of resveratrol-based carbon dots with high biocompatibility for in situ screening inhibitors on amyloid protein aggregation and scavenging reactive oxygen species

coredata.dc:description: It is important to monitor the aggregation process of amyloid protein for fundamental researches, diagnosis and drug development of the related diseases, like neurodegenerative and metabolic diseases. Resveratrol (Res) has attracted great attention due to its high medical values. However, the application of Res is seriously restricted, because of its poor bioavailability caused by low water solubility and poor absorption. To address the problem, for the first time, this study put forward a novel strategy to nano-derivatize Res to carbon dots, i.e. Res-DES-CDs, based on the ternary deep eutectic solvent (DES). DES played the roles to assist the solubility and carbonization of Res, and improve biocompatibility. Res-DES-CDs possessed the highest water compatibility as reported so far for Res related derivatives. Meanwhile, Res-DES-CDs retained some functionalities of Res, thus exhibiting the turn-on fluorescence probing ability towards amyloid fibrils without disturbing amyloid aggregation; it was applicable to in situ screen aggregation inhibitors in real time. In addition, Res-DES-CDs possessed better ability of scavenging reactive oxygen species than Res, but with lower cytotoxicity and higher cell uptake rate, which held a promise to alleviate oxidative stress in relative diseases. The proposed strategy could be generalized to enhance water solubility and biocompatibility of compounds of poor solubility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of insulin fibrils by Res-DES-CDs. (A) The fluorescence response of Res (10 μg mL-1) towards insulin monomers (ins-M) and fibrils (ins-F, 10 μM). (B) The fluorescence response of Res-DES-CDs (10 μg mL-1) towards insulin monomers and fibrils (10 μM). (C) The effect of Res-DES-CDs concentrations (1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200, 500 μg mL-1) on F/F 0 and F towards insulin fibrils (10 μM). (D) The fluorescence spectra of Res-DES-CDs (1 μg mL-1) towards insulin fibrils (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 20 μM). Insert: A linear relationship by plotting F/F 0 versus concentration of insulin fibrils (0.2–20 μM). Data are presented as mean values±SD, n = 3 (C and insert of D).

In vitro monitoring insulin aggregation by Res-DES-CDs. (A) Kinetics of insulin aggregation using ThT (i) and Res-DES-CDs (ii) as probes. (B) Kinetics of insulin aggregation incubated with 1 μg mL-1 (iii-a), 10 μg mL-1 (iii-b) and 50 μg mL-1 (iii-c) of Res-DES-CDs. (C) The Congo red assay of the product at the ending point corresponding to curves (i), (iii-a), (iii-b) and (iii-c). CR: the fluorescence spectrum of Congo red. CT: the fluorescence spectrum of Congo red and insulin fibrils without Res-DES-CDs. (D) The TEM image of the final product of curve (i). (E) The TEM image of the final product of curve (iii-b). Data are presented as mean values±SD, n = 3 (A and B), and the kinetic curves were fitted by Boltzmann growth curve according to the mean values.

Screening protein aggregation inhibitors. (A) The kinetic curve of aggregation with the addition of high concentration (1 mg mL-1) of EGCG, florfeniol, betaine, L-ascorbic acid, dopamine, epinephrine, terbutaline, atropine, hydrocortisone. (B) The kinetic curve of low concentration (10 μg mL-1) of these drugs. Data are presented as mean values±SD (n = 3) (A and B). The kinetic curves in (A and B) were fitted by Boltzmann growth curve according to the mean values.

ROS scavenging assays. (A) The clearance ratio of O2 •− by Res (1, 5, 10, 20 μg mL-1) and Res-DES-CDs (20, 40, 60, 80, 100 μg mL-1) by the NBT assay. ChCl/TEG represents the direct mixture of ChCl and TEG with the molar ratio of 1:4. (B) The clearance ratio of DPPH• by Res and Res-DES-CDs (10, 25, 50, 100 μg mL-1). (C) The clearance ratio of ABTS+ • radicals by Res and Res-DES-CDs (0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 μg mL-1). (D) The clearance ratio of •OH radicals by Res and Res-DES-CDs (1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 μg mL-1). Data are presented as mean values±SD, n = 3 (A, B, C, D).

Scavenging intracellular ROS produced by insulin fibrils. (A) The bright field, green light channel and merged photos of SH-SY5Y cells stimulated by insulin fibrils and treated by Res or Res-DES-CDs. (B) The fluorescence intensity quantified by Image J and statistical analysis of insulin fibril-related ROS scavenging in (A). Data are presented as mean values±SD, n = 3 (B), * P < 0.05, * * P < 0.01 and * ** P < 0.001, determined by a one-way ANOVA; NS, not significant.
The effects of tested drugs of different concentrations on kinetics parameters of insulin aggregation.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: 1% and 0.01% of DMSO was in the same volume as 1 mg mL-1 and 10 μg mL-1 of drugs, respectively.

head.ce:title: Deep eutectic solvent-assisted preparation of resveratrol-based carbon dots with high biocompatibility for in situ screening inhibitors on amyloid protein aggregation and scavenging reactive oxygen species

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is important to monitor the aggregation process of amyloid protein for fundamental researches, diagnosis and drug development of the related diseases, like neurodegenerative and metabolic diseases. Resveratrol (Res) has attracted great attention due to its high medical values. However, the application of Res is seriously restricted, because of its poor bioavailability caused by low water solubility and poor absorption. To address the problem, for the first time, this study put forward a novel strategy to nano-derivatize Res to carbon dots, i.e. Res-DES-CDs, based on the ternary deep eutectic solvent (DES). DES played the roles to assist the solubility and carbonization of Res, and improve biocompatibility. Res-DES-CDs possessed the highest water compatibility as reported so far for Res related derivatives. Meanwhile, Res-DES-CDs retained some functionalities of Res, thus exhibiting the turn-on fluorescence probing ability towards amyloid fibrils without disturbing amyloid aggregation; it was applicable to in situ screen aggregation inhibitors in real time. In addition, Res-DES-CDs possessed better ability of scavenging reactive oxygen species than Res, but with lower cytotoxicity and higher cell uptake rate, which held a promise to alleviate oxidative stress in relative diseases. The proposed strategy could be generalized to enhance water solubility and biocompatibility of compounds of poor solubility.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The protein misfolding and amyloid aggregation are closely related to neurodegenerative diseases (e.g. Alzheimer’s disease (AD)) and metabolic diseases (e.g. type II diabetes) [1,2]. In the occurrence and development of these diseases, amyloid aggregation happens and causes a series of problems, including neuroinflammation, metal ions imbalance and oxidative stress [1–3]. Since no effective drugs are available so far, it remains a global issue to cure these diseases all along. Thus, it is very important to monitor the aggregation process of amyloid proteins for fundamental researches, diagnosis and drug development of the amyloid-related diseases. Besides, the amyloid-induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) can cause oxidative stress damage and lead to neurofibrillary tangle formation, metabolic dysfunction and cognitive decline [4]. Therefore, releasing the oxidative stress would be a promising way to relieve symptoms.

Resveratrol (Res), a polyphenol widely present in traditional Chinese medicine and plant foods, has attracted great attention, owing to its high medical values of antioxidation and antitumor activities [5]. Recently, Res was reported to have the potential for the AD therapy via multiple mechanisms, including sirtuin passage, scavenging ROS and chelating metal ions, etc [6–8]. In addition, Res responded to aggregated amyloid proteins with enhanced fluorescence, and was considered as a complementary fluorescence probe to determine amyloid proteins quantitatively [9,10]. Unfortunately, the application of Res was seriously restricted, due to its poor bioavailability caused by the low water solubility and bad absorption [11]. To expand the applicability of Res, some strategies should be employed to improve the properties.

In this study, we propose a novel approach to nano-derivatize Res to carbon dots (CDs). CDs are a species of zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials with remarkable fluorescence properties, good biocompatibility, fantastic water solubility, low toxicity, environment-friendliness, high availability and low cost, etc [12–17]. CDs are usually obtained from carbon sources in the suitable media with prescribed preparation conditions, and possibly inherit some properties from the carbon source [18]. For example, recently, CDs were especially popular in drug/gene delivery with non-toxicity and the ability to across blood-brain barrier, implying the potential for AD treatment [19–21]. Herein, Res was the carbon source, and thus the generated CDs were highly expected to possess some properties of Res of concern, especially like scavenging ROS and fluorescence response to amyloid fibrils.

In the nano-derivatization of Res, to assist the carbonization of Res and improve the biocompatibility further, deep eutectic solvent (DES) was deliberately selected as both the synthetic media and the doping reagent. DES, a new generation of “green solvent”, is usually obtained by quaternary ammonium salts coordinated with metal salts or interacted with hydrogen bonded donors (HBD) [22,23]. As an alternative solvent to traditional organic ones, DES possesses excellent water solubility but with no volatility and less toxicity. For example, the DES based on choline chloride (ChCl) and HBDs was demonstrated to extract effective ingredients from natural medicinal plants more efficiently than some traditional organic solvents [24]. Secondly, DES can enhance solubility and permeability of drugs for promoting the drug absorption and efficacy. The bioavailability of several water-insoluble drugs, i.e. ibuprofen, curcumin, acetaminophen, lidocaine and some protein drugs, was greatly enhanced using the ChCl-based DES as the solvent medium [25]. Thirdly, DES can play the dual roles as the reaction medium and the doping reagent for producing CDs. Generally, CDs synthesized in the DES showed good fluorescence performance, low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility [26,27]. As ChCl is a common component of DES, in most cases, Cl and N co-doped CDs were resulted for multiple applications [26].

Herein, using the DES to assist the preparation of CDs, the novel Res-based carbon dots, named as Res-DES-CDs, were obtained. The ternary DES based on ChCl, triethylene glycol (TEG) and Res was used, in which active gradient, i.e. Res (ROS scavenger and fluorescence reporter), was directly involved as a part of DES. Thus, the formation and carbonization of DES could be achieved in one pot, which was different from the previous methods associated with several steps [23,24]. Res-DES-CDs were characteristic of the highest water compatibility as reported so far for Res related derivatives, exhibited the turn-on fluorescence probing ability towards amyloid fibrils, and scavenged ROS with low cytotoxicity and high cell uptake rate. As Res-DES-CDs responded to fibrils very fast without interfering the aggregation process, they were the effective probe for in situ monitoring amyloid aggregation in real time, and particularly suitable to screen the inhibitors on the amyloid aggregation for the high-throughput purpose. Meanwhile, Res-DES-CDs could effectively eliminate ROS induced by amyloids, and were regarded as a potential candidate for alleviating oxidative stress. More importantly, the construction strategy of Res-DES-CDs could be generalized to improve the solubility of some insoluble active gradients of natural products, catering for diagnosis and treatment of related diseases.

The DES based on ChCl and TEG (named as 2-DES) was prepared according to the reported method [28]. Briefly, ChCl and TEG with a molar ratio of 1:4 were mixed and stirred at 100 ℃ for 2 h until a homogeneous liquid phase was obtained, i.e. 2-DES.

Res-DES-CDs were prepared via two methods, i.e. one-pot and two-step methods. For the one-pot method, 50 mg (0.22 mmol) of Res, 1.06 g (7.6 mmol) of ChCl and 4.05 mL (30.4 mmol) of TEG were mixed and stirred at 120 ℃ for 2–10 h. For the two-step method, 50 mg of Res was added into 5.0 mL of the 2-DES with stirring at 120 ℃ for 2–10 h. To be noted, the addition of Res had an ignorable interference on the volume of DES, either for the 2-DES or Res-DES-CDs. To better compare the properties of Res and Res based nanomaterials, we used the content of Res to stand for the concentration of Res-DES-CDs. Therefore, in the one-pot method, for the total volume of 5.0 mL of Res-DES-CDs, the concentration of Res-DES-CDs was denoted as 10 mg mL-1 in terms of the concentration of Res.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn-on fluorescence probing of amyloid fibrils by Res-DES-CDs
Evaluation of fluorescence probing performance of Res-DES-CDs towards amyloid fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: At the optimized detection condition obtained in S1.4, Supporting Information, the linear relationship was established between F/F 0 and the concentration of insulin fibrils (0.05–20 μM). The LOD was calculated by 3σ/k, in which σ represents relative standard deviation of 11 blank samples and k represents the slope of the linearity equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro monitoring of amyloid aggregation process
ce:section.ce:para: The insulin monomers (40 μM) were incubated in the HCl-KCl solution (50 mM) at 65 ℃ for 1 h with constant stirring at 300 rpm. During the incubation, 200 μL of the incubation solution was sucked out at the prescribed time point (0, 1, 3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 min) and divided into two equals. One aliquot of the sucked solution was diluted with 900 μL PB buffer (10 mM, pH=7.4) containing 11.1 μM thioflavin T (ThT), and then subjected to the fluorescence intensity measurement (λ ex=430 nm, λ em=488 nm) to obtain aggregation kinetic curve (i). In parallel, the other aliquot was diluted with 900 μL H2O containing 1.1 μg mL-1 Res-DES-CDs, and then subjected to the fluorescence intensity measurement (λ ex=330 nm, λ em=380 nm) to obtain aggregation kinetic curve (ii).

To test the in situ monitoring ability of Res-DES-CDs towards the amyloid aggregation process, Res-DES-CDs of different concentrations (1, 10, 50 μg mL-1) were directly incubated with the insulin monomers (40 μM) in the HCl-KCl solution (50 mM) at 65 ℃ for 1 h with constant stirring at 300 rpm. At the prescribed time, the fluorescence intensity of the system was measured (λ ex=330 nm, λ em=380 nm) to obtain aggregation kinetic curves (named as iii-a, iii-b, iii-c) corresponding to 1, 10 and 50 μg mL-1 Res-DES-CDs in this order.

The kinetic sigmoid curve of the insulin aggregation was fitted using the following Eq. (1):
(1)
Y=Y 0+(Y max-Y 0)/[1+exp-(t-t 1/2)k]

Y represents the fluorescence intensity recorded at the time of t; Y 0 and Y max signify the normalized fluorescence intensity of the initial insulin monomers and ultimate insulin fibrils, respectively.

To evaluate the interference of Res-DES-CDs on the aggregation, the products at the ending point was analyzed by the Congo red assay and TEM observation. The products at the ending point corresponding to curves (i), (iii-a), (iii-b) and (iii-c) were separately added with Congo red (10 μg mL-1) for 20 min incubation, and then the fluorescence spectra were scanned (
λ
e
x
max ⁡
=524 nm) to tell the degree of insulin aggregation [18]. For the TEM observation, 1 μL of products was dropped onto the copper mesh and washed by H2O for three times. After drying naturally, the sample on the mesh was for the TEM observation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ screening inhibitors on insulin aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: (-)-Epigallocatechin gallatewere (EGCG) was adopted to test the effectiveness of the in situ screening system. Florfeniol, betaine, L-ascorbic acid, dopamine, epinephrine, terbutaline, atropine and hydrocortisone were test drugs to screen their inhibitory ability on aggregation of insulin. These drugs were firstly tested whether they interfered the fluorescence of Res-DES-CDs or not. The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture of insulin fibrils (10 μM) and Res-DES-CDs (1 μg mL-1) with each of the studied drugs (1 mg mL-1) or the same volume of DMSO (as the drugs were prepared in DMSO) in the HCl-KCl solution (50 mM, pH=2) were scanned (λex=330 nm). The spectra remained unchanged, satisfying the prerequisite for the in situ screening.

To screen the inhibitors, the insulin monomers (40 μM), Res-DES-CDs (1 μg mL-1) and each of the tested drugs of two concentrations (1 mg mL-1 and 10 μg mL-1), were incubated in the HCl-KCl solution (50 mM, pH=2) at 65 ℃ for 9 h without stirring. DMSO of the same volume instead of the tested drugs was used as the blank control and EGCG of the same concentrations was as a positive control. The fluorescence intensity (λex=330 nm, λem=380 nm) at different time points was determined by the Microplate Reader. The kinetic sigmoid curve was also fitted using Eq. (1). The inhibition efficiency (%) was calculated by Eq. (2):

To test scavenging ability of Res-DES-CDs towards the intracellular ROS induced by amyloid aggregation, insulin fibrils (20 μM) were added into cell culture medium to stimulate cells to produce ROS. SH-SY5Y cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at the density of 104 cell per well, incubating at 37 ℃ and 5% CO2 for 16 h. Afterwards, the cells were incubated with the DMEM containing insulin fibrils, insulin fibrils+Res (2 μg mL-1) or insulin fibrils+Res-DES-CDs (2 μg mL-1) for another 4 h, and insulin fibrils in these groups were all 40 μM. After the cells were washed by the DMEM, DCFH-DA (20 μM) was added and incubated for another 30 min. After gently washed by DMEM, the cells were observed and photographed with a fluorescence microscope, and the fluorescence intensity was quantified by Image J. The clearance ratio of intracellular ROS was calculated by Eq. (3):
(3) Intracellular ROS Clearance Ratio=(F ROS-F Test)/F ROS×100%
F ROS represents the fluorescence intensity of insulin fibril groups, and F Test represents the fluorescence intensity of Res or Res-DES-CDs treated groups.

Other experimental details, including the materials and instruments, preparation of other materials, calculation of hydrogen bonding energy, optimization of fluorescence probing conditions towards amyloid fibrils by Res-DES-CDs, the fluorescence response of Res and Res-DES-CDs towards solvent viscosity and polarity, determination of K d, molecular docking, ROS scavenging in vitro, cytotoxicity studies, the cell uptake experiment, hemolytic assay, histological analysis and statistical analysis, are described in Supporting Information.

Res is well known for its antioxidation. However, its application is seriously restricted, owing to its poor water solubility (3 mg per 100 mL in water) [29]. Although the solubility of Res in some organic solvents is enhanced, e.g. EtOH (50 mg mL-1), DMSO (16 mg mL-1), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) (∼65 mg mL-1) and acetone (50 mg mL-1) [29], these solvents cannot be compatible with biological systems. Thus, the water solubility of Res remains as a bottleneck for its wide applications all along. As an alternative solvent to the traditional organic solvents, DES is a “green” solvent with no or less toxicity, good water solubility and biocompatibility, and reported to promote dissolution of natural products of poor water solubility [24]. In our experiment, the solubility of Res in the 2-DES (shortened as RD solution) was found to exceed 100 mg mL-1 at the room temperature; to the best of our knowledge, it was the highest solubility of Res related derivatives as reported so far. However, despite that 2-DES was miscible with water, Res would still precipitate, when the RD solution was dispersed to large amount of water, especially containing Res of the high concentration. This phenomenon indicates that the direct use of the RD solution could not yet be suitable for biological systems. In order to endow Res with good water compatibility, derivatization of Res to be CDs, i.e. Res-DES-CDs, could be a solution if the properties of Res can be retained or improved.
The optimization of synthetic conditions and characterization of Res-DES-CDs were discussed in S2, Supporting Information.

According to the characterization results, Res-DES-CDs retained the residual functional groups of Res, including stilbene and phenolic hydroxyl groups, and were thus supposed to reserve some properties of Res, e.g. antioxidation and responding to amyloid fibrils with turn-on fluorescence. In addition, Res-DES-CDs were found to own N- and Cl- containing functionalities, which could be originated from ChCl and TEG, demonstrating the role of the DES as the dopant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The turn-on fluorescence properties of Res-DES-CDs towards amyloid fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: In our preliminary experiment, we found that Res-DES-CDs behaved similarly to Res ( Fig. 1A and B), showing no response towards insulin monomers, but extremely turning on the fluorescence in the presence of insulin fibrils. However, 2-DES-CDs obtained without Res (S1.2, Supporting Information) exhibited no response towards insulin fibrils (Fig. S6, Supporting Information), illustrating the irreplaceable role of Res for turning on the fluorescence responding to fibrils. As Res-DES-CDs possessed the “turn-on” fluorescence selectivity toward fibrils, it was attempted as the fluorescent probe for fibrils detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of insulin fibrils using Res-DES-CDs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: The detectability of Res-DES-CDs towards fibrils was investigated taking insulin fibrils as the example. Incubation time was found to influence the detection sensitivity less (data not shown), while the concentration of Res-DES-CDs influenced much on the response (Fig. 1C). The highest F/F 0 value as well as the high F value was obtained in the case of 1 μg mL-1 of Res-DES-CDs, which was chosen as the optimal concentration. At this detection condition, the response (F/F 0) was increased with the elevating concentration of fibrils from 0.05 μM to 20 μM (Fig. 1D), and a good linearity between F/F 0 and concentration of insulin fibrils was obtained in the range of 0.2 μM and 20 μM (Fig. 1D insert) with an LOD of 36 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro monitoring insulin aggregation using Res-DES-CDs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: Insulin was a general model protein to study the aggregation of amyloids because of its typical β-sheet structure, low price and fast aggregation [30]. Based on the quick response of Res-DES-CDs to insulin fibrils as discussed above, it was attempted as a fluorescent probe to monitor the aggregation of insulin. ThT, the gold standard fluorescence probe for in vitro monitoring protein aggregation, was adopted for comparison. As depicted in Fig. 2A, curve (i) and curve (ii) was obtained using ThT and Res-DES-CDs as the respective probe; the t 1/2 was calculated to be 9.47 ± 0.26 min and 10.74 ± 0.12 min, and the t lag was 6.18 ± 0.50 min and 6.23 ± 0.36 min, respectively. The t 1/2 and t lag measured by Res-DES-CDs and ThT were close to each other, demonstrating the applicability of Res-DES-CDs as the probe to monitor the protein aggregation in vitro.

Moreover, Res-DES-CDs were found to influence the aggregation a little. When Res-DES-CDs (1, 10, 50 μg mL-1) were directly co-incubated with the insulin, the curves (iii-a, b, c) in Fig. 2B were obtained, and successfully fitted by the “S” shape growth curve approaching the curves (i) and (ii). Also, as listed in the insert of Figs. 2A and 2B, the t 1/2 and t lag obtained from these curves were comparable, especially at the low concentration of Res-DES-CDs, 1 μg mL-1 and 10 μg mL-1.

To further confirm ignorable influence of Res-DES-CDs on the protein aggregation, the product at the ending point of insulin aggregation was analyzed by the Congo red staining and transmission electron microscopic (TEM) observation. As displayed in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence intensities of the final products in the case of insulin, and insulin co-incubated with Res-DES-CDs of different concentrations were almost identical to each other, indicating that the degree of insulin aggregation with and without Res-DES-CDs was almost identical. Additionally, the TEM images revealed the similar morphology of the final products at the ending point of the aggregation under different conditions (Fig. 2D and E). These results fully reflect that Res-DES-CDs would not affect the aggregation of proteins and held the potential for in situ monitoring amyloid proteins in real time. As we know, although ThT was the gold standard fluorescent probe for in vitro monitoring protein aggregation, it was unsuitable for in situ monitoring in real time, as it was claimed to interfere the aggregation [31–34]. From this point, Res-DES-CDs were superior to ThT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ screening protein aggregation inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: Up to now, effective drugs for AD were still unavailable, and new drug development for the AD is still on the way. Amyloid aggregation inhibitors could be potential to treat the disease, and screening amyloid aggregation inhibitors is important for the AD drug development [35]. Thus, in situ monitoring the amyloid aggregation for high-throughput screening inhibitors in real time is highly desired. As discussed above, Res-DES-CDs had ignorable interference to the protein aggregation with a promise for this purpose.

First of all, the applicability of Res-DES-CDs as the in-situ probe to screen the inhibitors was verified using EGCG, a recognized inhibitor for amyloid aggregation, as a model drug. As depicted in Fig. S7, Supporting Information, the fluorescence of Res-DES-CDs turned on by insulin fibrils was not interfered by EGCG. Thus, the insulin monomers, Res-DES-CDs and EGCG of two concentrations (1 mg mL-1 and 10 μg mL-1) were co-incubated, and the fluorescence was monitored at the prescribed time. As depicted in Fig. 3A and B, obviously, EGCG could both delay the aggregation (prolonging t lag and t 1/2 as calculated in Table 1) and reduce the aggregation degree (weakened fluorescence) at both two studied concentrations; these two effects were more remarkable at the higher concentration of 1 mg mL-1 than those at lower concentration of 10 μg mL-1, with the inhibition efficiency of 77.1% and 26.2%, respectively. The inhibition efficiency of EGCG obtained herein was comparable to that of reported [36], which illustrated the effectiveness of Res-DES-CDs as an in-situ probe to screen inhibitors.

The in-situ screening system was then used to screen inhibitors among the old drugs or natural products according to the idea of “old medicine for new use”. Herein, owing to their satisfactory anti-inflammatory, antioxidation or other appealing efficacy, the widely-used one of nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs (florfeniol), and two natural products (betaine and L-ascorbic acid), as well as two neurotransmitter (dopamine and epinephrine), a β2-receptor stimulant (terbutaline), an anticholinergic agent (atropine), and one of glucocorticoids (hydrocortisone) were chosen as the test drugs to study their inhibitory effect on insulin aggregation by Res-DES-CDs [37–39]. The growth curves for aggregation are depicted in Fig. 3A and B, the t 1/2, t lag and maximum relative FL intensity, and the inhibition efficiency are listed in Table 1.

For florfeniol, the comparable inhibition efficiency of 30.2% and 26.5% for simultaneously delaying and lessening the aggregation at both high (1 mg mL-1) and low concentration (10 μg mL-1) were obtained, illustrating that florfeniol may be a potential inhibitor irrelevant of the concentration in the certain range. As a contrast, betaine could lessen the aggregation at high concentration (1 mg mL-1) with the inhibition efficiency of 37.9% (P < 0.001), but the effect was not significant at low concentration (10 μg mL-1); meanwhile, betaine at both two concentrations did not delay the aggregation process of insulin. The result indicates that the inhibitory effect of betaine was concentration dependent. In addition, L-ascorbic acid could slightly lessen the degree of insulin aggregation at both high and low concentrations, with the inhibition efficiency of 13.5% and 22.2% (P < 0.001), respectively, but could not delay the insulin aggregation. Dopamine exhibited no inhibitory effect but promoted the aggregation process. Terbutaline and epinephrine exhibited comparable inhibition efficiency at high and low concentration, possibly due to their similar structure. Atropine and hydrocortisine exhibited respective inhibition efficiency of 21.0% and 17.3% at high concentration, but nearly no inhibitory effect at low concentration.

Above all, florfeniol, L-ascorbic acid, epinephrine and terbutaline could be the potential inhibitors towards protein aggregation, the inhibition efficiency of betaine, atropine and hydrocortisine were concentration dependent, while dopamine accelerated aggregation. For their clear biosafety and efficacy, the screened old drugs except dopamine might be possible candidate drugs for further study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of turn-on fluorescence of Res-DES-CDs towards amyloid fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of the turn-on fluorescence of Res-DES-CDs towards amyloid fibrils was discussed from the perspective of the response to the microenvironment, the binding affinity and molecular docking. During the formation of amyloid fibril, the polarity and viscosity of the microenvironment may change correspondently. As reported, with the enhanced micro-environmental viscosity accompanying the protein aggregation, the rotation of styrene structure of the Res molecule was restricted, and thus the conjugate plane was enlarged and the fluorescence was enhanced [40]. Meanwhile, with the aggregation of the protein, hydrophobic β-sheet of the protein aggregates was increasingly available; thus, owing to the benzene ring structure in Res, Res is feasible to interact with the β-sheet part of amyloid aggregates through hydrophobic interaction, which could also restrict the rotation of styrene in Res and strengthen the fluorescence. Considering that Res-DES-CDs reserved the styrene group from Res (Section 3.1), the fluorescence enhancement of Res-DES-CDs towards fibrils may rely on the similar reasons. Hence, the response of Res-DES-CDs towards polarity and viscosity was firstly investigated. As shown in Fig. S8A, both Res and Res-DES-CDs had the lowest fluorescence intensity in H2O. When the polarity of the solution decreased, strengthened fluorescence was observed for both Res and Res-DES-CDs correspondently. As shown in Fig. S8B, both Res and Res-DES-CDs responded to the elevated viscosity with the obviously strengthened fluorescence intensity. These phenomena demonstrate that Res-DES-CDs inheriting the structure of Res responded similarly towards the microenvironment, including viscosity and polarity, which may be a contributing factor for the “turn-on” fluorescence of Res-DES-CDs towards fibrils.

Next, the binding affinity between insulin fibrils and Res-DES-CDs was evaluated by calculating their K d values [41]. The K d values of ThT, Res and Res-DES-CDs with insulin fibrils were obtained as 1.87 μg mL-1, 0.77 μg mL-1 and 0.86 μg mL-1 (Fig. S8C, D and E), respectively, which revealed that both Res-DES-CDs and Res exhibited stronger binding affinity to insulin fibrils than ThT. Although Res-DES-CDs were slightly weaker than Res in terms of the affinity to insulin fibrils, they were comparable, as the K d values were in the same order of magnitude.

Moreover, molecular docking was carried out to illustrate the interaction between Res-DES-CDs and fibrils. As the molecular weight of insulin fibrils was too large, it was difficult to realize molecular docking operation directly using insulin fibrils [42]. As well, the exact structure of Res-DES-CDs was unknown. Thus, β-amyloid (Aβ) fibril and Res molecule were instead used for simulation for reference. The results disclosed that Res was encapsulated in the cavity of β-sheet structure, and hydrogen bonds were formed between phenolic hydroxyl groups of Res molecule and LEU-34, MET-35, VAL-36 of Aβ (Fig. S8F). As Res-DES-CDs had similar phenolic hydroxyl groups as Res (Section 3.1), it could be speculated that Res-DES-CDs may also interact with fibrils through these sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro ROS scavenging by Res-DES-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The aggregation of amyloids could cause intracellular oxidative stress, and the excessive ROS would damage nerve cells and tissues. Hence, eliminating ROS could be a potential approach to treat neurodegenerative diseases [43]. Res was well known for its scavenging ROS ability [8]. Considering that Res-DES-CDs possessed similar functionalities to Res as discussed above, Res-DES-CDs were supposed to possess the ROS scavenging ability. The scavenging ability of Res-DES-CDs was then evaluated towards superoxide anion radicals (O2 •−), 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl radicals (DPPH•), 2,2'-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) radicals (ABTS+•) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH), respectively. The nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) assay was employed to evaluate O2 •− scavenging ability of Res-DES-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 4A, both ChCl/TEG and 2-DES nearly could not eliminate O2 •−, but both Res and Res-DES-CDs exhibited concentration dependent behavior to eliminate O2 •−. However, as the aqueous medium was used in this assay and Res had limited water solubility, the highest O2 •− clearance ratio of Res reached only 14.1% at almost the largest solubility of 20 μg mL-1 in the PB solution. On the contrary, as the water dispersibility of Res-DES-CDs was largely enhanced as discussed above, the high concentration of Res-DES-CDs could be possibly up to 100 μg mL-1. As a result, the O2 •− clearance ratio of Res-DES-CDs was further remarkably enhanced, with the highest clearance ratio up to 68.3% in the case of 100 μg mL-1 Res-DES-CDs. Half effective concentration (EC50), i.e. the concentration of the scavenger when the clearance rate reaches 50%, of Res-DES-CDs towards O2 •− was 58 μg mL-1. Obviously, thanks to the improved water dispersibility, Res-DES-CDs exerted stronger ability to eliminate O2 •− than Res.

Furthermore, the DPPH• and ABTS+• assays were used to evaluate the total antioxidation ability. As the DPPH• assay was carried out in EtOH, both Res and Res-DES-CDs can be used in a high concentration. As depicted in Fig. 4B, the clearance ratio of DPPH• was increased with the increasing concentration of Res or Res-DES-CDs, while neither ChCl/TEG nor 2-DES could eliminate DPPH•. Res-DES-CDs exhibited slightly stronger DPPH• clearance ability than Res, but both with ∼70% DPPH• clearance ratio at the concentration of 100 μg mL-1. The EC50 values of Res and Res-DES-CDs towards DPPH• were 46 μg mL-1 and 32 μg mL-1, respectively (Fig. 4B). Similarly, both Res and Res-DES-CDs exerted strong ABTS+• clearance ability. Even at the relatively low concentration of 10 μg mL-1, both of the ABTS+• clearance ratio reached up to 90%. The EC50 of Res and Res-DES-CDs towards ABTS+• was absolutely identical (2.5 μg mL-1) (Fig. 4C). However, ChCl/TEG and 2-DES could not eliminate ABTS+•.

HRP is an enzyme which could catalyze H2O2 to generate •OH, and then oxidize TMB to generate oxTMB. HCl is a terminator which could break HRP and oxTMB turned yellow at acidic condition with the absorption at 450 nm. As shown in Fig. 4D, ChCl/TEG and 2-DES could not eliminate •OH, while Res and Res-DES-CDs exhibited comparable •OH scavenging activity, with the EC50 of 3.1 and 2.7 μg mL-1, respectively.

The above results suggested the excellent antioxidation activities of Res-DES-CDs. Owing to its excellent water dispersibility, compared to Res, Res-DES-CDs owned the superior O2 •− and DPPH• clearance ability, and comparable ABTS+• and •OH clearance abilities. Additionally, the antioxidation activity of Res-DES-CDs was comparable to other derived Res materials [44,45]. As reported, the resveratrol nanofibers scavenged ABTS+• with the EC50 of more than 10 μg mL-1 [44]. Another Res-encapsulated lipophilic protein nanoparticles had the optimal DPPH• clearance ratio of 67.1% [45].

Moreover, the mechanism of Res-DES-CDs scavenging ROS was considered to be similar as Res, in light of the residual Res structure on Res-DES-CDs. It has been elaborated that the main way of scavenging ROS by polyphenols was through hydrogen atom transfer (ArOH+R•→ArO•+RH) and the single electron transfer (ArOH+R•→ArOH+•+R-) [46]. The products ArO• and ArOH+• are more stable radicals with low reactivity, because the odd electron on aromatic structures has the possibility to be spread over the entire molecule [47]. The phenolic hydroxyl groups were the main antioxidation function groups while Res-DES-CDs reserved phenolic hydroxyl groups from Res as discussed above, thus, Res-DES-CDs could work similarly as Res.

The ROS scavenging ability of Res-DES-CDs was investigated in cell. In Fig. 5A, insulin induced obvious cell oxidative stress as the green fluorescence of 2',7'-dichlorofluorescein (DCF) greatly increased. As the Fig. 5B showed, the fluorescence intensity of the insulin stimulated group was 319.6% of that of the control. As a contrast, fluorescence of cells treated with Res and Res-DES-CDs was significantly reduced to 118.2% and 35.6% of that of control, with the ROS clearance ratios of 63.0% and 88.9%, respectively. Obviously, Res-DES-CDs behaved a little better than Res when dealing with the ROS in cell, which further demonstrates the better antioxidation activity of Res-DES-CDs than the Res. This property may be owing to the enhanced water dispersibility, and thus the improved bioavailability and cell uptake [48].
The cytotoxicity, cell uptake rate and the biosafety of Res-DES-CDs were discussed in S3 and S4, Supporting Information.

In this study, the ternary DES based on choline chloride, triethylene glycol and Res was directly carbonized via the one-step approach to generate Res-DES-CDs. In light of appreciated properties of DES, which could assist the solubility and carbonization of Res, and improve biocompatibility, the Res-DES-CDs were demonstrated to possess the highest water compatibility as reported so far for Res related derivatives; meanwhile, Res-DES-CDs retained some functionalities of Res, thus exhibiting the turn-on fluorescence probing ability of amyloid fibrils, and scavenging ROS but with low cytotoxicity and high cell uptake rate. Especially, Res-DES-CDs did not disturb the amyloid aggregation, and could be used as the fluorescence probe for in situ monitoring the aggregation process and thus screening the aggregation inhibitors in real time for high-throughput purpose. This would be a useful tool to assist searching the potential drugs for the diseases. However, as only the blue-purple fluorescence was turned on, Res-DES-CDs were unsuitable for the imaging in vivo. In addition, as Res-DES-CDs possessed superior properties to Res, like excellent antioxidation ability and the enhanced water compatibility, it held the promise to alleviate oxidative stress in relative diseases.

Additionally, the proposed nano-derivatization strategy was to transfer the carbon source (resveratrol) to carbon dots (Res-DES-CDs), to enhance the water dispersibility and biocompatibility of Res while retain the properties of turn-on fluorescence response towards amyloid fibrils and antioxidation even much better. Additionally, this nano-derivatization could be realized in one step, avoiding tedious reaction steps involved in the small molecules’ modification. This strategy may be generalized to address the problems of such compounds with poor water solubility and limited biocompatibility, and the related research was still going on.

Wan-chun Luo: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ming Jiang: Methodology. Li Li: Methodology. Li-na Bao: Methodology. Xu Yu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Li Xu: Funding acquisition, Supervision Writing – review & editing. All authors have read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Miss Wan-Chun Luo is a PhD student in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. Her research topic focuses on the novel fluorescent nanomaterial for diagnosis and treatment of neurodegenerative diseases.

Xu Yu received his Ph.D. degree from Wuhan University in 2013. He worked as a postdoctoral researcher at the Department of Biomedical Engineering, Pennsylvania State University, USA and the Department of Chemistry, Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Israel, respectively. Currently, he is an associate professor in the Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. His current research focused on microfluidic chip, biosensors, and nanotechnology.

Prof. Li Xu is a full professor in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. She received her PhD degree from National University of Singapore in 2009. Her research interests focus on separation and analysis of complex samples and application of functional materials in pharmaceutical, medical and environmental analysis.
